rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the verbatim text: it targets reduced facility energy consumption via incentives and cost/savings allocation. Quotes: 'The cost effects of energy consumption and energy savings will be better allocated for the actors affecting the energy consumption of facilities' and 'develop a method that encourages all actors to conserve energy.' This aligns with Resource Efficiency (energy_use_reduction in buildings). The gate failure ('no Resource Efficiency outcome token') appears to be a tokenization issue because the outcome is stated as 'conserve energy' and 'energy savings'. Climate screen is 'explicit_self', reinforcing keep.
policy_statement: Better allocate energy cost and savings impacts to actors influencing facility energy consumption and develop an encouraging method for all actors to conserve energy.
mechanism_description: Better allocating energy costs and savings to the actors influencing facility consumption is intended to encourage energy conservation and reduce building energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service (comfort/operations) → improved incentives/behavior → ↓ energy input → ↓ upstream GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Adjust allocation of energy bills, budgets, and savings (e.g., to tenants, departments, facility operators) so decision-makers responsible for consumption face the cost and/or receive the benefit of reductions.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced energy demand through conservation actions (thermostat setpoints, schedules, plug-load management, operational tuning) and potentially accelerated uptake of low/zero-cost efficiency measures.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower delivered energy (electricity, district heat, gas, oil) use in buildings → reduced combustion and power-sector generation → ↓ GHG emissions (and often ↓ peak load).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption, which lowers associated GHG emissions from electricity and heating/cooling energy supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: incentive alignment → reduced energy use for the same service → lower energy supply emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the direct lever is reducing energy input via efficiency/behavior rather than primarily switching fuels (Mitigation) or reducing hazard exposure (Adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions). Emissions reductions occur downstream from reduced energy demand.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a specific policy instance primarily reallocates costs to fund on-site renewables or fuel switching, its dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation—but that is not inherent to this canonical mechanism, which targets demand reduction through cost/savings allocation.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy that better allocates energy costs/savings to actors influencing consumption is, by design, an energy-demand reduction mechanism (resource efficiency) regardless of building type or governance context.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., behavioral efficiency vs. operational controls vs. contracting model) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified as Mitigation when bundled with explicit fuel-switching/renewable procurement requirements; however, the core mechanism is demand reduction via incentive alignment, which fits Resource Efficiency.
key_indicators: “cost effects of energy consumption and energy savings will be better allocated”; “actors affecting the energy consumption”; “develop a method that encourages all actors to conserve energy”
co_benefits: Operational cost savings; improved energy management/accountability
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via internal pricing/cost-center charging (pricing-mechanism) or via governance/planning rules; the excerpt does not specify the mechanism, so it is classified broadly as an incentive-program/enabling approach focused on conservation.
